notes: Swat et al. 2012 CC3D tutorial. Moderate homotypic adhesion. Range 3-8.
Foty & Steinberg 2005. Strong homotypic adhesion via cadherins. Range 1-3. Measured by tissue surface tensiometry.
Foty & Steinberg 2005. Weaker than epithelial. Range 5-8.
Foty & Steinberg 2005. Endothelial cells form adherens junctions. Range 3-6.
Glazier & Graner 1993. Heterotypic adhesion weaker than homotypic. Drives cell sorting. Range 8-14.
Glazier & Graner 1993, GGH model convention. High J = unfavorable = cells avoid floating in void. Range 12-20.
Swat et al. 2012. Strong adhesion (low J). Fibroblasts are collagen-native. Range 2-5.
Estimated. GelMA retains RGD motifs from gelatin; fibroblast adhesion comparable to collagen. Range 3-6.
Foty & Steinberg 2005. MSCs show strong integrin-mediated adhesion to collagen. Range 2-5.
Estimated. MSCs adhere to GelMA via RGD but less avidly than to native collagen. Range 5-10.
Paluch et al. 2015. Endothelial cells strongly adhere to fibronectin via α5β1 integrin. Range 2-4.
Estimated. HUVECs prefer fibronectin over gelatin-derived matrices. Range 6-10.
HepG2 moderately adherent to collagen. Range 5-10. Less integrin expression than mesenchymal cells.
Estimated. PCL is hydrophobic; cells attach but weakly without surface treatment. Range 8-14. Plasma treatment or protein coating improves adhesion.
Estimated. PDMS is highly hydrophobic; very weak cell adhesion without surface modification. Range 14-20.
Caliari & Burdick 2016. Unmodified alginate is biologically inert — no cell adhesion ligands. Cells form spheroids instead of attaching. Range 12-18.
RGD-modified alginate restores integrin-mediated adhesion. Range 4-8 depending on RGD density.
Matrigel is rich in laminin and collagen IV — excellent for epithelial cell adhesion. Range 3-7.
Murphy & O'Brien 2010. Moderate-strong adhesion. Range 4-8.
Fibroblasts strongly adhere to fibrin and actively remodel it. Range 2-5.
MSCs express CD44 and bind HA. Moderate adhesion without integrin ligands. Range 4-8.
Foty & Steinberg 2005. Direct measurement. Surface tension σ = 22.8 ± 3.5 mJ/m². Higher σ = stronger cohesion.
Foty & Steinberg 2005. σ = 14.0 ± 1.9 mJ/m². Weaker than fibroblasts.
Foty & Steinberg 2005. σ = 56.0 mJ/m². Very strong epithelial cohesion. Drives enveloping during gastrulation.
Measured by confocal microscopy
MMP-2 (gelatinase A). Range 1-15 pg/cell/day. Upregulated by mechanical stress and growth factors (TGF-β). Major ECM remodeling enzyme.
Lower than activated fibroblasts. Range 0.5-5 pg/cell/day. Increases during chondrogenic differentiation.
Zaman et al. 2006. Highly invasive cancer line; high MMP secretion. Range 10-50 pg/cell/day combined MMP-2/MMP-9.
Wolf et al. 2013. Below ~7 µm pore size, cells require MMP activity to enlarge pores. Above this, amoeboid migration possible without proteolysis.
Zaman et al. 2006. Range 5-20 µm/h depending on collagen density. Biphasic: optimal speed at intermediate stiffness (~1 mg/mL).
Wolf et al. 2013. Range 2-15 µm/h. Speed depends on pore size relative to nuclear diameter. Below ~3 µm pores, nuclear deformation becomes rate-limiting.
Ehrbar et al. 2011. Range 10-40 µm/h in loose gels. Fibroblasts are fast migrators in permissive matrices.
Ehrbar et al. 2011. Dense collagen slows migration by restricting pore space.
Collective migration (tip-stalk). Range 5-15 µm/h. Sprout elongation rate rather than single-cell speed.

conditions: 37°C, 2.5 mg/mL fibrin, wound healing assay in 3D
notes: MSCs migrate readily in fibrin due to plasmin-mediated degradation. Range 8-25 µm/h.
Estimated. GelMA at 5% has smaller pore sizes (~50 µm) than dilute collagen, limiting migration. Cells must degrade GelMA via MMPs to move.
Alginate is non-degradable and lacks adhesion ligands. Cells cannot migrate through intact alginate — they are trapped. Migration ~0 unless alginate is modified or degrading.
Wolf et al. 2013. Range 10-15 µm. Critical parameter for migration through confined spaces. Nuclear stiffness is rate-limiting below ~7 µm pore size.
Zaman et al. 2006. Biphasic relationship: too sparse = insufficient traction, too dense = physical obstruction. Optimal ~1-2 mg/mL for most mesenchymal cells.
McMurtrey 2016, Table 1. Canonical reference value. Multiple independent measurements converge on 2.8–3.2e-5. Used as baseline for fractional reduction in hydrogels.
Cheema et al. 2012. Exact value from abstract: 4.5e-6 cm²/s. Measured with optical fibre-based O₂ sensor embedded in core + Fick's law model.
Cheema et al. 2012. Exact value from abstract: 1.7e-6 cm²/s. Same method as do2_002.
Cheema et al. 2012. Exact value from abstract: 3.4e-6 cm²/s. Crosslinking reduced D vs uncrosslinked 11% (4.5e-6).
Colom et al. 2014. Measured D_O2 in collagen, fibrin, and Matrigel. 'Oxygen diffusivity in acellular gels was up to 40% smaller than that of water.' Range for dilute collagen ~1.8–2.4e-5 cm²/s.
Colom et al. 2014. Denser ECM gels showed lower diffusivity. Up to 40% reduction from water for densest gels. Matrigel is denser than dilute collagen.
Mehmetoglu et al. 1996. Range 2.54–2.58e-5 cm²/s in alginate gel beads. Close to water, indicating alginate at low concentration is highly permeable to O₂.

conditions: 37°C, internal gelation, PyroScience O₂ probe + COMSOL fitting
notes: Orimi et al. 2025/2026. Range ~1.5–2.0e-5. Alginate source (Manugel vs Protanal) affects D significantly due to guluronate/mannuronate ratio differences.

conditions: 37°C, internal gelation, PyroScience O₂ probe + COMSOL fitting
notes: Orimi et al. 2025/2026. Range ~2.5–3.0e-5. Higher than Manugel variant — near water-like diffusivity.
Orimi et al. 2025/2026. Measured 3.2 ± 0.5e-5 cm²/s. Confirms historical reference values.
Valentin et al. Range 1.9–3.1e-5 cm²/s. Decellularized dermal ECM retains near-water diffusivity.
Valentin et al. Range 0.7–2.0e-5 cm²/s. More variable than Alloderm due to multilayer structure.
Hulst et al. 1989. Range 1.5–2.1e-5 across 0.5–5% w/v. Decreases with concentration.
NO PUBLISHED MEASUREMENT EXISTS for GelMA D_O2. Estimated: >90% water content at 5% GelMA suggests D close to water. Range estimate 2.0–2.5e-5. CRITICAL GAP — measurement needed.
NO PUBLISHED MEASUREMENT EXISTS. Estimated: ~85% water at 10% GelMA, denser network. Range estimate 1.5–2.0e-5. Needs direct measurement.
Wagner et al. 2011 consensus. Range 0.5–5.0e-3 mol/m³. Use Michaelis-Menten kinetics (Q = Vmax × [O₂] / (Km + [O₂])) for thick constructs where local pO₂ may drop below ~5 mmHg.

Wagner et al. 2011. Range 200–400 amol/cell/s. Confirmed independently by Place et al. 2017 (DOI 10.1016/j.freeradbiomed.2017.10.003): 'Primary hepatocytes ... consuming 200–400 attomoles (amol)/cell/s.' One of the highest OCR values of any mammalian cell type.
Wagner et al. 2011. Cross-validated: Clark electrode (25°C) ~32.5 and Seahorse XF96 (37°C) ~35. Range 28–38 amol/cell/s.
Wagner et al. 2011. Seahorse XF24.
Wagner et al. 2011. Range 63–88 amol/cell/s measured by EPR oximetry and respirometer.
Wagner et al. 2011. Range 17–28 amol/cell/s. Method-dependent: optical O₂ quenching vs Seahorse XF24.
Wagner et al. 2011. Clark electrode. Range 12.5–27 amol/cell/s.
Wagner et al. 2011. 3.06e-17 mol/s/cell = 30.6 amol/cell/s.
~98 fmol/cell/hr × 0.278 = ~27.2 amol/cell/s. Respirometry measurement.
Deep zone Vmax ~6.6 fmol/cell/hr = ~1.8 amol/cell/s. Superficial zone ~3.2 fmol/cell/hr = ~0.9 amol/cell/s. Range 0.3–1.8. Highly glycolytic cells. Clark electrode.
Wagner et al. 2011. From abstract: 'average rate of oxygen utilization of 2.5 × 10⁻¹⁸ mol cell⁻¹ s⁻¹, i.e., 2.5 amol cell⁻¹ s⁻¹' based on 10¹⁴ cells consuming 22 mol O₂/day.
Wagner et al. 2011. Originally reported as protein-normalized; converted to per-cell using estimated protein content. Range 30–80 amol/cell/s. Lower than primary hepatocytes.
Reported as 37 ± 12 amol/cell/s from meta-analysis (n=2630 wells). Seahorse XF96 at 37°C.

cell_type: BMSCs (bone marrow stromal cells, day 0 in 3D)
conditions: 20% O₂, agarose encapsulated, FE modeling from O₂ probes
notes: 43.2 ± 6.2 amol/cell/s. OCR drops to 13.1 ± 0.6 amol/cell/s by day 24 of chondrogenic differentiation (TGF-β3).
13.1 ± 0.6 amol/cell/s. Dramatic decline from 43 at day 0 — reflects metabolic shift during chondrogenic differentiation.
Range 2.1–4.8 amol/cell/s. Immature iPSC-CMs rely on glycolysis. Much lower than primary neonatal rat CMs (40–100 amol/cell/s). Maturation shifts OCR upward.

Cells suspended in construct
Freshney 'Culture of Animal Cells'. Range 3-5 × 10^4 cells/cm². Classic contact-inhibited line.
Freshney. Range 1-2 × 10^5. Transformed line, weak contact inhibition, can pile up.
Range 5-8 × 10^5. Forms tight polarized monolayer at confluence. Differentiates post-confluence.
Range 5-8 × 10^4. Contact-inhibited endothelial monolayer.
Estimated from typical confluent density. HEK293 are loosely adherent and can detach at high density.
ATCC product sheet CRL-1573. Suspension-adapted variant. Adherent HEK293 in 10% FBS/DMEM can be faster (18-24h).
ResearchGate user reports; consistent with Sen Lab protocol. HEK293T grows faster than parental HEK293 due to SV40 T antigen.

conditions: 37°C, 5% CO2, 10% FBS, EMEM or DMEM, log phase
notes: ATCC CCL-2 product sheet. Well-established canonical value. Range 20-28h depending on media and passage.

conditions: 37°C, 5% CO2, 10% FBS, MEM + NEAA + insulin, log phase
notes: Cytiva/HyClone study measured 40-60h. ATCC ACC-115 consistent. Clumpy growth complicates counting.
ATCC CRL-1658. DSMZ ACC-59. Range 20-26h. Contact-inhibited; must passage before confluence.
Pattappa et al. 2011. Range 30-40h varies with donor, passage, and FBS lot. bFGF supplementation accelerates.
Terminally differentiated CMs rarely divide. Proliferation index <1% in mature cultures. For CC3D, set proliferation probability to ~0.
Primary hepatocytes are quiescent in standard culture. No meaningful doubling time. HGF/EGF can induce limited proliferation. For CC3D, set division rate to 0.
ATCC HB-8065. Range 24-30h. Grows in clusters; enzymatic dissociation needed for accurate counts.
Lonza/PromoCell specifications. Range 18-24h. Highly passage-dependent; senescence onset P6-P8.
ATCC HTB-37. Range 40-62h. Requires 20% FBS. Differentiates to enterocyte-like phenotype at confluence.

conditions: 37°C, 5% CO2, 10% FBS, F-12K or DMEM, log phase
notes: ATCC CCL-185. Range 20-24h. Robust grower.
ATCC CCL-61. Range 14-20h depending on media. CHO-S suspension variant may differ.
ATCC HTB-14 reports ~34h. Measured at 30.8 ± 2.5h by Tzedakis et al. 2017.
ATCC CCL-34. Range 20-26h. Epithelial, contact-inhibited.
Breslin & O'Driscoll 2013. 3D spheroid growth rate about 2× slower than 2D monolayer. Outer proliferative rim, quiescent core.
Nichol et al. 2010. Estimated from viability/proliferation data in GelMA. ~2× longer than 2D MSC doubling.
ATCC CCL-81. Range 12-18h. Important for vaccine production.
Highly variable by donor age and zone of origin. Range 48-96h in monolayer. De-differentiate rapidly in 2D.
ATCC CRL-2539. Fast-growing, aggressive triple-negative breast cancer model. Range 18-24h.
Measured by SEM imaging
Measured by confocal microscopy
Measured by confocal microscopy

conditions: 37°C, collagenase II (0.5 U/mL) or cell-secreted MMPs
notes: Yue et al. 2015. Range 7-14 days at 3%. Higher concentrations degrade slower (28-56 days at 10%). Enzymatic degradation.
Caliari & Burdick 2016. Range 3-7 days at 5 mg/mL. Rapid degradation by plasmin. Aprotinin slows this.
Antoine et al. 2014. Range 7-14 days at 2 mg/mL. Cell-density dependent.
Drury & Mooney 2003. Range 4-8 weeks. Bulk erosion. Acidic degradation products can affect cell viability.
Yue et al. 2015. Range 30-80 µm at 5%. Decreases with concentration. Relevant for cell migration.
Murphy & O'Brien 2010. Optimal pore size 85-120 µm for osteoblast/MSC attachment. Larger pores (325 µm) showed less initial attachment but better long-term infiltration.
Yue et al. 2015 comprehensive review. Range 1-3 kPa. Compressive modulus by unconfined compression.
Nichol et al. 2010. Range 3-8 kPa. Compressive modulus.
Yue et al. 2015. Range 15-30 kPa. Higher crosslink density at 10%.
Antoine et al. 2014. Range 0.5-2 kPa. Rheometry (storage modulus G').
Antoine et al. 2014. Range 2-5 kPa.
Caliari & Burdick 2016. Range 0.5-2 kPa at 5 mg/mL.
Caliari & Burdick 2016. Range 1-3 kPa.
Caliari & Burdick 2016. Range 5-15 kPa. Tunable by Ca²⁺ concentration and alginate MW.
Caliari & Burdick 2016. Range 0.1-0.5 kPa. Very soft, reconstituted basement membrane.
Drury & Mooney 2003. Range 300-400 MPa. 3D printed scaffolds have lower effective modulus due to porosity.
Range 1-3 GPa bulk, ~1-3 MPa for porous scaffolds. Degrades via hydrolysis.
Standard recipe. Range 0.5-3 MPa tunable by ratio. Non-degradable.
Tibbitt & Anseth 2009. Range 2-50 kPa tunable by MW and concentration. Non-degradable unless MMP-cleavable crosslinker added.
Caliari & Burdick 2016. Range 0.5-100 kPa tunable by modification and crosslinker.
Range 0.1-100 kPa depending on concentration and processing. Highly tunable. Beta-sheet content increases stiffness.

Measured by Live/Dead assay

Measured by confocal microscopy
Yue et al. 2015. Range 1.3-1.5 at 3%. Decreases with concentration.
Yue et al. 2015. Range 1.1-1.2 at 10%.
